items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: How do glaciers form U-shaped valleys?
items.correct: Glaciers erode existing V-shaped river valleys through plucking (freezing onto rock and pulling pieces away) and abrasion (rock embedded in the ice scraping the valley floor and sides).
items.misconception: Glaciers carve U-shaped valleys from flat land by pushing through.
items.random_wrong: U-shaped valleys form when rivers freeze and expand.
items.why: Glaciers usually deepen pre-existing river valleys, not create them from nothing. Students miss that glacial landscapes inherit and modify earlier fluvial topography.

items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: What glacial landform is a KNIFE-EDGE RIDGE between two corries?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Arête. “Pyramidal peak” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which erosion process involves the glacier freezing onto rock and tearing it away?
items.why: It's Plucking. “Abrasion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many thousand years ago was the last UK glacial maximum?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
